scenarios.name: silent_collision
scenarios.response_text:
**B) events**

Both logs say the migrations for webhook tracking and API v2 delivery lifecycle **“Alters the `events` table”**, including adding `webhook_status` and `delivery_state` (plus `processor_version`).

scenarios.name: calendar_blindspot
scenarios.response_text:
**C) Sarah — she only works Monday, Wednesday, Friday.**  
On Thursday March 13, Sarah is unavailable because her contract schedule is M/W/F only (and her stated PTO is March 13 anyway).

scenarios.name: unasked_question
scenarios.response_text: **B)** SAML SSO — It was explicitly scoped out of the auth migration (“No SAML in this phase … would add 3 weeks”), even though Josh told Acme it would come after.

scenarios.name: misread_metric
scenarios.response_text: **B.** The flows no longer measure the same thing: the new onboarding removed steps (including the former “first milestone” trigger). So “completion” in the new flow isn’t directly comparable to “completion” in the old flow, making the 60% → 85% comparison misleading.

scenarios.name: budget_interpreter
scenarios.response_text: **C** — `staging-3` was created for load testing but **never actually used** for that purpose (last deploy Jan 22), making it the intended-but-unused staging environment.

scenarios.name: three_ticket_pattern
scenarios.response_text: **B** — All three billing issues involve customers from **migration batch-7, run 2026-02-25**. That’s the only shared, concrete connection across the wrong invoice amount, slow billing page, and incorrect plan display.

scenarios.name: meeting_assassin
scenarios.response_text: **C)** — Only **webhook payload format** is still unresolved. The meeting notes say there are **2 open questions** in the design doc, but for **pagination** it records **“Cursor vs offset — Sarah recommends cursor, Alan agrees”** (consensus), leaving just the **webhook payload: nested vs flat** as the remaining open question.

scenarios.name: first_day_briefing
scenarios.response_text: **B)** `src/billing/mapping.rs` — this is the “rosetta stone file” that maps between legacy `plan`/`tier`/`subscription` naming conventions.

scenarios.name: scope_surgeon
scenarios.response_text: **B.** Josh’s research shows **all 3 enterprise prospects said CSV is sufficient**. **PDF wasn’t requested by any customer**, and the **Excel request came from the sales rep**, not from the customers.

scenarios.name: green_test_trap
scenarios.response_text: **C.** The workspace shows **0 edge case tests** and **7 end-to-end tests** in the suite (both explicitly listed).

scenarios.name: thread_therapist
scenarios.response_text: **B)** Long-lived JWT tokens with bearer auth — it preserves the security benefits (rotation/revocation) of JWT while keeping the developer experience closer to “API key simplicity,” which matches Sarah’s proposed middle ground in the thread.

scenarios.name: silent_failure_premortem
scenarios.response_text: **C** — The webhook system has **no delivery confirmation mechanism**. So even if the customer endpoint returns **200** but doesn’t actually process the webhook, both sides will assume success, and the failure can stay silent until invoices/integration reconciliation breaks weeks later.

scenarios.name: timezone_play
scenarios.response_text: **B) Takeshi at NovaTech in Tokyo** — the workspace notes he offered to review the proposal from an API consumer perspective and typically responds within 2 hours, and no one has asked the customer perspective yet.

scenarios.name: debt_ledger
scenarios.response_text: **C** — About **2.75 hours per week** total (1h hardcoded URLs + 45m retry logic + 1h generic error messages + additional missed-bug risk from the skipped webhook test).

scenarios.name: competitor_signal
scenarios.response_text: **C)** 2 prospects (Acme Corp and Pinnacle Inc) asked about Ed25519 in the last two weeks, and it’s cheap for us because Sarah’s webhook code already has a signature module—adding a new algorithm would be localized there (about half a day).

scenarios.name: roi_translator
scenarios.response_text:
**B**

Break-even occurs at **feature 5** (per the workspace math: without = 25 days, with = 25 days at N=5). That means the refactor becomes **net positive starting from feature 6 onward**, i.e., “paying for itself” around **feature 4/5 and clearly after**. Among the options, **B** best matches the workspace’s “break-even at feature 5; net positive from feature 6 onward” framing.

scenarios.name: reverse_escalation
scenarios.response_text: **B)** The root cause is a **spec ambiguity** in `docs/billing-display-spec.md`: “subscription start date” can mean either the **signup date (Jan 15)** or the **billing period start (Feb 1)**. Engineering implemented the billing-period interpretation, so the fix is essentially a **1-line UI label change** (or show both dates), not a backend/data bug.

scenarios.name: lunch_decision
scenarios.response_text: **B.** Adding annual billing would require changes to proration/refunds and dunning logic, which would directly interfere with the **March 31 Billing Migration (Phase 2)** cutover—there’s no buffer if that slips.

scenarios.name: postmortem_reframe
scenarios.response_text: **B** — Monitoring detected the anomalies in **12 minutes**, and the root cause was a **process gap**: the script was not tested against production-scale data with full historical records (legacy accounts missing `plan_change_date`).

scenarios.name: rate_limit_ghost
scenarios.response_text: **C)** Normal combined usage is **95 req/sec**, which is **95%** of the **100 req/sec** limit. During traffic spikes (typically **1.5–2x**), that becomes **143–190 req/sec**, which **exceeds the limit** and leads to **Stripe 429 errors** / customer-facing failures.
